title: Board Oversight of Technology Risk
translation: The board must actively oversee how the institution manages technology risks. This means technology risk should be part of the overall enterprise risk framework, and the board must ensure enough budget, people, and tools are dedicated to managing it.
evidence: Board meeting minutes discussing technology risk
Enterprise risk management framework incorporating technology risk
Board-approved technology risk budget and resource allocation
Board terms of reference covering technology risk oversight

artifacts.description: Formal charter defining board responsibilities for technology risk oversight

artifacts.description: Periodic dashboard report presented to the board on technology risk posture

title: Board Technology Risk Committee
translation: You need a dedicated board committee focused on technology risk. Its members must understand technology and cyber topics, and it must meet at least every quarter to review the current state of technology risk.
evidence: Board committee charter or terms of reference
Committee member profiles demonstrating relevant expertise
Quarterly meeting schedules and attendance records
Committee meeting minutes and action items

title: Board Competency in Technology
verbatim: The board must designate a board-level committee which shall be responsible for supporting the board in providing oversight over technology-related matters. The composition of the designated committee must include at least one member with technology experience and competencies.
translation: Board members should either have personal expertise in technology and cybersecurity, or have access to advisors who do. Ongoing training and education programs should keep the board current on new threats and technology trends.
evidence: Board training records on technology and cybersecurity topics
Annual training plan for board members

artifacts.description: Records of board training sessions on technology and cybersecurity

artifacts.name: Board Competency Framework
artifacts.description: Framework defining expected technology competencies for board members

artifacts.name: Board Competency Assessment Report
artifacts.description: Annual assessment of board members' technology knowledge and skill gaps

title: Board Risk Appetite Statement
verbatim:
To promote effective discussion at the board level amidst a rapidly evolving technology and cyber risk landscape, the board must-
(a) obtain regular updates on technology risk and cyber threats;
(b) allocate sufficient time to discuss cyber risks, including the strategic, reputational and liquidity risks and impact of operational disruption to stakeholders which could arise from an extreme cyber incident. This shall be supported by input from external experts where appropriate; and
(c) participate in relevant cybersecurity awareness and training programmes.
translation: The board must formally approve a statement that sets out how much technology risk the institution is prepared to tolerate. This should include both measurable metrics and qualitative descriptions, and must be reviewed yearly or when circumstances change significantly.
evidence: Evidence of risk appetite communication to management

artifacts.name: Technology Risk Appetite Statement
artifacts.description: Board-approved statement defining acceptable levels of technology risk

artifacts.name: Risk Appetite Review Records
artifacts.description: Evidence of annual review and board sign-off on risk appetite

artifacts.name: Risk Appetite Monitoring Report
artifacts.description: Periodic report on actual risk levels versus approved appetite thresholds

title: Board Reporting on Technology Risk
verbatim: The board audit committee (BAC) shall be responsible for ensuring the effectiveness of the internal technology audit function. This shall include ensuring the adequate competence of the audit staff to perform technology audits. The BAC shall review and ensure appropriate audit scope, procedures and frequency of technology audits. The BAC shall also ensure effective oversight over the prompt closure of corrective actions to address technology control gaps.
translation: Management must give the board regular, detailed reports on technology risk at least every quarter. Reports must cover KRIs, incident trends, remediation progress, and forward-looking risk assessments so the board can make informed decisions.
evidence: Quarterly technology risk reports to the board
Incident summary reports presented to board

artifacts.name: Quarterly Board Risk Report
artifacts.description: Comprehensive quarterly technology risk report presented to the board

artifacts.name: KRI Dashboard
artifacts.description: Key risk indicator dashboard with trend analysis

artifacts.name: Board Acknowledgement Records
artifacts.description: Evidence that the board received and discussed technology risk reports

title: Senior Management Accountability
verbatim:
The senior management shall bear primary responsibility for the day-to-day management of technology risks including cyber risks. In fulfilling its responsibilities, senior management must–
(a) implement board approved TRMF and CRF into specific policies and procedures that are consistent with the approved risk appetite and risk tolerance;
(b) ensure that the crisis management plan for service disruptions provides for timely escalation of recovery action, taking into consideration the impact on customers, such as:
(i) duration of disruption;
(ii) number of customers affected by a disruption; and
(iii) number and value of financial transaction impacted; and
(c) provide regular updates to the board on the status of key performance indicators with pertinent information on the risk controls to facilitate informed performance review.
translation: Senior management is directly responsible for putting the board-approved technology risk framework into practice. They must appoint a CISO (or equivalent) who can report directly to the board or its technology risk committee on cybersecurity issues.
evidence: Senior management accountability matrix for technology risk
CISO appointment letter and job description
Board or committee meeting records showing CISO reporting

artifacts.name: Senior Management Accountability Matrix
artifacts.description: Document defining senior management roles and accountability for technology risk

artifacts.name: CISO Appointment and Mandate
artifacts.description: Formal appointment of CISO with defined mandate and reporting lines

artifacts.name: CISO Board Reporting Records
artifacts.description: Records of CISO presentations or reports to board or committee

title: Technology Risk Management Function
verbatim:
The senior management must establish a cross-functional committee to provide guidance on the financial institution’s technology plans and operations. The members of the committee must include senior management from both cyber and technology functions, as well as major business units. The committee’s responsibilities shall include the following:
(a) oversee the formulation and effective implementation of the strategic technology plan and associated technology policies and procedures;
(b) provide timely updates to the board on key technology matters; and
(c) approve any deviation from technology-related policies after having carefully considered a robust assessment of related risks. Material deviations shall be reported to the board.
translation: You must have a dedicated technology risk management team that operates independently from the IT operations team. This team needs proper authority, staffing, and access to systems and data to do its job of identifying, assessing, monitoring, and reporting technology risks.
evidence: Organisation chart showing independence from IT operations

artifacts.name: Technology Risk Function Charter
artifacts.description: Charter defining the mandate, authority, and independence of the technology risk function

artifacts.name: Organisation Structure Chart
artifacts.description: Organisational chart demonstrating independence of risk function from IT operations

artifacts.name: Staffing and Competency Records
artifacts.description: Staffing plan and competency matrix for technology risk function personnel

title: Technology Risk Framework
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that the TRMF is an integral part of the financial institution’s enterprise risk management framework (ERM).
translation: You must create and maintain a technology risk management framework that fits your institution's size and complexity. It should cover governance structures, risk appetite, how you assess risks, and how you report on them.
evidence: Framework alignment with enterprise risk management

artifacts.name: Technology Risk Management Framework
artifacts.description: Comprehensive framework document covering governance, assessment, and reporting

artifacts.name: Board Approval Record
artifacts.description: Evidence of board approval of the technology risk management framework

artifacts.name: Framework Review Report
artifacts.description: Annual review report on the effectiveness and currency of the framework

title: Risk Identification & Assessment
translation: You must regularly and systematically identify and assess technology risks by looking at threats, vulnerabilities, how likely they are, and what impact they could have. This applies to all critical technology assets and must happen at least yearly or whenever there are major changes.
evidence: Risk assessment schedule and completion records
Evidence of assessment upon significant change events

artifacts.name: Risk Assessment Methodology
artifacts.description: Documented methodology for identifying and assessing technology risks

artifacts.name: Technology Risk Assessment Report
artifacts.description: Completed risk assessment report covering all critical assets

artifacts.name: Risk Assessment Schedule
artifacts.description: Schedule showing planned and completed risk assessments

title: Risk Treatment & Mitigation
verbatim:
A financial institution must establish an independent enterprise-wide technology risk management function which is responsible for-
(a) implementing the TRMF and CRF;
(b) advising on critical technology projects and ensuring critical issues that may have an impact on the financial institution’s risk tolerance are adequately deliberated or escalated in a timely manner; and
(c) providing independent views to the board and senior management on third party assessments, where necessary.
translation: For every identified technology risk, you need a treatment plan: avoid it, mitigate it, transfer it, or accept it. Risk acceptance requires formal sign-off by someone with appropriate authority. Each plan must have deadlines and a named person responsible.
evidence: Risk treatment plans with assigned owners
Risk acceptance approvals at appropriate authority level

artifacts.name: Risk Treatment Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining risk treatment options and approval authorities

artifacts.name: Risk Treatment Plan Register
artifacts.description: Register of all risk treatment plans with owners, timelines, and status

artifacts.name: Risk Acceptance Records
artifacts.description: Formal risk acceptance approvals signed by appropriate authority

title: Risk Monitoring & Reporting
translation: You should set up ongoing monitoring and regular reporting of technology risks. Define KRIs that are tracked and reported to management and the board. When risk thresholds are breached, there should be automatic escalation processes.
evidence: Key risk indicator definitions and thresholds

artifacts.name: Risk Monitoring Procedures
artifacts.description: Procedures for continuous monitoring and reporting of technology risks

artifacts.name: KRI Monitoring Dashboard
artifacts.description: Dashboard or report showing key risk indicators and their status

artifacts.name: Escalation Procedures
artifacts.description: Documented escalation procedures for risk appetite threshold breaches

title: Technology Risk Register
verbatim:
The CISO shall be responsible for ensuring that the financial institution’s information assets and technologies are adequately protected, which includes-
(a) formulating appropriate policies for the effective implementation of TRMF and CRF;
(b) enforcing compliance with these policies, frameworks and other technology-related regulatory requirements; and
(c) advising senior management on technology risk and security matters, including developments in the financial institution’s technology security risk profile in relation to its business and operations.
translation: You must keep a complete, current register of all technology risks. Each entry needs the risk description, likelihood, impact, owner, treatment plan, and status. This register must be reviewed at least every quarter to ensure it reflects reality.
evidence: Technology risk register with all required fields
Quarterly review records of the risk register

artifacts.name: Technology Risk Register
artifacts.description: Comprehensive register of all identified technology risks with assessments and treatment plans

artifacts.name: Quarterly Review Records
artifacts.description: Evidence of quarterly review and update of the risk register

artifacts.name: Risk Owner Sign-off Records
artifacts.description: Records of risk owners acknowledging their assigned risks

title: IT Project Governance
verbatim: A financial institution must establish appropriate governance requirements commensurate with the risk and complexity of technology projects undertaken. This shall include project oversight roles and responsibilities, authority and reporting structures, and risk assessments throughout the project life cycle.
translation: You need a formal governance framework for IT projects with clear roles, stage-gate approvals, and oversight. Major projects must undergo an independent risk assessment and get board or senior management approval before they start.
evidence: Project approval records with stage-gate sign-offs
Independent risk assessment reports for major projects
Board or senior management approval records

title: Project Risk Assessment
translation: Every IT project must have a risk assessment covering security, availability, and data integrity. You also need to consider how the project interacts with existing systems and what impact it could have on customers and day-to-day operations.
evidence: Project risk assessment template and methodology
Risk mitigation plans for identified project risks

title: Post-Implementation Review
verbatim: The board and senior management must receive and review timely reports on the management of these risks on an ongoing basis throughout the implementation of significant projects.
translation: After major IT projects go live, you should conduct a formal review within three months to check if objectives were met, capture lessons learned, and evaluate whether risk controls are working as intended.

artifacts.description: Review report assessing project outcomes and lessons learned

artifacts.name: Lessons Learned Register

artifacts.name: Risk Control Effectiveness Assessment
artifacts.description: Assessment of whether implemented risk controls are operating effectively

title: SDLC Framework
translation: You must use a formal SDLC framework where security is built into every phase from requirements through to maintenance. Each phase must have defined security checkpoints and quality gates that must be passed before proceeding.
evidence: Quality gate criteria and sign-off records
Evidence of framework adherence in recent projects

title: Requirements Management
verbatim:
A financial institution must adopt a methodology for an effective and secure implementation of IT systems. Key phases of System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) shall include requirement, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance and decommissioning, and integrate with-
(a) enterprise architecture to ensure successful execution of business strategy;
(b) risk management policies and practices to achieve business objectives; and
(c) security principles and requirements to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer and counterparty information.
translation: Security and compliance requirements must be clearly documented at the start of every development project. These requirements must be traceable through design, build, and test so you can prove each one was implemented and validated before go-live.

artifacts.name: Requirements Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for capturing, tracing, and validating security requirements

artifacts.name: Requirements Traceability Matrix
artifacts.description: Matrix tracing security requirements through development phases to validation

artifacts.name: Requirements Validation Records
artifacts.description: Records demonstrating validation of security requirements prior to deployment

title: Secure Development Standards
verbatim: A financial institution must meet enterprise security, governance and compliance requirements when using rapid system development methodology. Given the dynamic environment can increase likelihood of errors, a financial institution shall automate the IT security compliance review to prevent unauthorised access as well as the discovery and testing of security vulnerabilities to ensure secure release of new IT services.
translation: You must have secure coding standards based on industry best practices like OWASP. All developers must be trained on them, and you must verify compliance through both manual code reviews and automated scanning tools.
evidence: Developer training records on secure coding
Code review records referencing coding standards
Automated scanning tool configuration and results

artifacts.description: Coding standards based on OWASP and industry best practices

artifacts.name: Developer Training Records

title: Code Review & Static Analysis
verbatim: A financial institution shall physically segregate the production environment from the development and testing environment to mitigate the risk of unauthorised changes to the production systems. Where a financial institution is relying on a cloud environment, the financial institution shall ensure that these environments are not running on the same virtual host.
translation: All code must go through both peer code reviews and automated static analysis (SAST) before it reaches production. Any critical or high-severity issues found must be fixed before release. If you cannot fix them, you need formal documented risk acceptance.
evidence: SAST tool configuration and scan results
Remediation records for critical and high findings

artifacts.description: Mandatory procedure for peer code reviews and SAST scanning

artifacts.name: SAST Scan Results
artifacts.description: Static analysis scan results for application releases

artifacts.name: Remediation Records
artifacts.description: Records of remediation of critical and high findings before release

title: Testing Standards
verbatim: A financial institution must establish a sound methodology for a rigorous system testing prior to deployment. The testing shall ensure that the system meets user requirements and performs robustly. Where sensitive test data is used, the financial institution must ensure proper authorisation procedures and adequate measures to prevent their unauthorised disclosure are in place.
translation: You must have formal testing standards covering functional, integration, performance, and security testing. Test plans must be documented upfront, and test results must be reviewed and approved before anything goes to production.
evidence: Test plan templates and completed test plans
Test execution results and defect reports

artifacts.description: Summary reports of testing outcomes and approval decisions

title: UAT & Acceptance
verbatim: The scope of system testing referred to in paragraph 10.8 may include unit testing, integration testing, user acceptance testing, application security testing, stress and load testing, regression, exception and negative testing, where applicable.
translation: Business users must conduct UAT in an environment that closely resembles production. Results must be formally documented and signed off by business owners before go-live. If production data is used in UAT, it must be properly masked or anonymised.
evidence: UAT environment specification and comparison to production
UAT sign-off records from business owners
Data masking procedures for UAT environments

title: Change Management Policy
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure any changes to the source code of critical systems are subject to adequate source code reviews to ensure code is secure and developed in line with recognised coding practices prior to introducing any system changes.
translation: Every change to production systems — applications, infrastructure, or configurations — must go through a formal change management process. Each change needs a risk assessment, testing, authorised approval, and must be logged in a change register.
evidence: Change advisory board terms of reference
Sample change records with risk assessment and approvals

title: Emergency Change Procedures
verbatim: A financial institution must establish appropriate procedures to independently review and approve system changes. The financial institution must also establish and test contingency plans in the event of an unsuccessful implementation of material changes to minimise any business disruption.
translation: You need specific procedures for handling urgent changes that cannot wait for the normal process. Emergency changes must be retrospectively reviewed and approved within a set timeframe, and management must receive reports on how often emergency changes are used.
evidence: Emergency change records with retrospective approvals
Management reports on emergency change frequency

title: Release Management
verbatim:
In relation to critical systems that are developed or maintained by third party service provider, a financial institution must, through contractual obligations, require third party service provider to:
(a) provide sufficient notice to the financial institution before any changes are undertaken that may impact the IT system;
(b) demonstrate that it adopts secure by design principles in IT system development methodology to mitigate cyber risks from propagating across the supply chain; and
(c) ensure the source code continues to be readily accessible for business continuity.
translation: You must have a structured release management process for deploying changes to production. Every release plan needs rollback procedures in case something goes wrong, verification steps to confirm the deployment worked, and clear criteria for what constitutes a successful release.
evidence: Sample release plans with rollback procedures

artifacts.description: Procedure for controlled deployment of changes to production environments

artifacts.name: Release Plans

title: Environment Separation
verbatim: When decommissioning critical systems, a financial institution must ensure minimal adverse impact on customers and business operations. This includes establishing and testing contingency plans in the event of unsuccessful system decommissioning.
translation: Development, testing, staging, and production must be clearly separated environments. Access to production must be tightly restricted, and moving code between environments must follow a formal, auditable process with proper controls.
evidence: Code migration procedures and audit trail

artifacts.description: Policy defining separation requirements between development, test, and production environments

artifacts.description: Audit trail of code migrations between environments

title: Source Code Protection
verbatim: A financial institution may deploy automated tools for software development, testing, software deployment, change management, code scanning and software version control to facilitate timely security assessment of critical systems in keeping with growing complexity in IT systems and emerging cyber threats.
translation: Source code must be protected from unauthorised access and changes. Use version-controlled repositories with proper access controls, track all changes, and ensure source code is never stored directly on production servers.
evidence: Evidence that source code is not in production environments

artifacts.name: Source Code Protection Policy

title: Configuration Management
verbatim:
Where a third party software is used, a financial institution should consider the potential risks and impacts a cyber supply chain incident may pose to its overall business operations and services. A financial institution may consider-
(a) adopting Software-Bill-of-Materials (SBOM) to automate the identification and continuous monitoring of potential security vulnerabilities including security issues associated with third party software components; and
(b) establishing open-source software security policy and procedures. This includes ensuring secure access to source code repositories in third party platforms, regular monitoring to prevent data leakages, adoption of secure coding practices, robust testing of open-source software and timely vulnerability assessment to mitigate security vulnerabilities and propagation of malwares across supply chain.
translation: You must manage configurations formally — keeping an accurate inventory of all technology assets and their settings. Establish approved configuration baselines and have processes to detect, assess, and fix any deviations from those baselines.

artifacts.description: Procedure for maintaining configuration baselines and managing changes

title: Capacity Planning
verbatim: A financial institution must develop and implement robust policies to identify and reduce shadow IT risks.
translation: You should plan technology capacity to ensure systems can handle current and future demand. Define capacity thresholds, monitor them continuously, and take action to scale up or procure more resources before limits are reached.

title: Patch Management Policy
translation: You must have a written patch management policy covering how patches are identified, evaluated, tested, and deployed. The policy must classify patches by severity and set maximum timeframes for each — for example, critical patches within 14 days, high within 30 days.
evidence: Patch deployment records and compliance metrics

title: Patch Deployment Timeline
translation: Critical patches must be applied within 14 days and high-severity patches within 30 days. If you truly cannot patch in time, you must implement compensating controls, document them, and get formal risk acceptance from the appropriate authority level.
evidence: Compensating control documentation for delayed patches
Risk acceptance records for patch exceptions

title: End-of-Life Management
verbatim:
A financial institution must continually monitor the effectiveness and security of the technology in use, incorporating developments in technology that may disrupt existing security controls. A financial institution shall-
(a) ensure its board receives advice on the potential impact to business operation arising from evolving technology landscape;
(b) formulate long-term strategy to address anticipated changes with allocation of competent resources to manage associated risks, including new cyber adversary tactics and techniques; and
(c) establish roadmap for system migration to preserve security and reliability of the technology infrastructure in an orderly manner.
translation: You must track all systems approaching or past end-of-life or end-of-support dates. Each one needs a migration or replacement plan. If an EOL system cannot be replaced yet, you need documented compensating controls and formal risk acceptance.
evidence: Migration or replacement plans for EOL systems
Compensating control documentation for legacy systems
Risk acceptance records for continued EOL system use

artifacts.name: EOL Migration Plans

artifacts.description: Formal risk acceptance and compensating controls for systems remaining past EOL

title: Cryptographic Policy
translation: You need a written cryptographic policy specifying which algorithms, key lengths, and protocols are approved for protecting data at rest and in transit. Align it with international standards (like NIST) and review it yearly to stay current with evolving threats.
evidence: Approved algorithm and key length specifications

title: Key Management
translation: Manage cryptographic keys through their full lifecycle — generation, distribution, storage, rotation, revocation, and destruction. For critical systems, keys must be stored in hardware security modules (HSMs) or similarly secure mechanisms.

artifacts.name: Key Management Framework
artifacts.description: Framework governing the full lifecycle of cryptographic keys

title: Encryption Standards
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure cryptographic controls are based on the effective implementation of suitable cryptographic protocols. The protocols shall include secret and public cryptographic key protocols, both of which shall reflect a high degree of protection to the applicable secret or private cryptographic keys. The selection of such protocols must be based on recognised international standards and tested accordingly. Commensurate with the level of risk, secret cryptographic key and private-cryptographic key storage and encryption / decryption computation must be undertaken in a protected environment, supported by a hardware security module (HSM), trusted execution environment (TEE) or similarly secured devices.
translation: All sensitive data must be encrypted both at rest and in transit. Use AES-256 or equivalent for symmetric encryption and at least RSA-2048 for asymmetric. Identify and replace any deprecated or weak algorithms still in use.
evidence: Inventory of encryption algorithms in use

artifacts.name: Encryption Standards
artifacts.description: Standards specifying minimum encryption requirements for data at rest and in transit

artifacts.name: Encryption Implementation Evidence

artifacts.name: Cryptographic Algorithm Inventory

title: Certificate Management
verbatim: A financial institution shall store public cryptographic keys in a certificate issued by a Certificate Authority, as appropriate to the level of risk. Such certificates associated with customers shall be issued by recognised Certificate Authorities. The financial institution must ensure that the implementation of authentication and signature protocols using such certificates are subject to strong protection to ensure that the use of private cryptographic keys corresponding to the user certificates are legally binding and irrefutable. The initial issuance and subsequent renewal of such certificates must be consistent with industry best practices and applicable legal / regulatory specifications.
translation: You must manage digital certificates through their lifecycle — issue, renew, revoke, and monitor them for expiry. Use trusted certificate authorities and monitor certificate transparency logs to detect any unauthorised certificates issued for your domains.

title: Data Centre Physical Security
verbatim: A financial institution must specify the resilience and availability objectives of its data centres to effectively support its business recovery objectives.
translation: Data centres must have multiple layers of physical security: perimeter fencing, multi-layer access controls (badges, biometrics), CCTV with at least 90 days of footage retention, and formal visitor management. Only authorised personnel may enter.
evidence: CCTV coverage maps and retention configuration

artifacts.description: Policy defining physical security requirements for data centre facilities

artifacts.description: Evidence of CCTV coverage and 90-day retention configuration

title: Environmental Controls
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure data centres have redundant capacity components and multiple distribution paths serving the computer equipment to eliminate any single point of failure for effective achievement of the identified business recovery objectives.
translation: Data centres need environmental controls for temperature, humidity, water leaks, and air quality. These must be monitored continuously with automated alerts when any parameter goes outside acceptable thresholds.

artifacts.name: Environmental Control Procedures
artifacts.description: Procedures for monitoring and managing data centre environmental conditions

artifacts.name: Environmental Monitoring Configuration
artifacts.description: Configuration of environmental monitoring systems and alert thresholds

artifacts.name: Environmental Monitoring Logs
artifacts.description: Continuous monitoring logs for temperature, humidity, and other environmental parameters

title: Power & Redundancy
verbatim: A financial institution shall host critical systems in a dedicated space intended for production data centre usage. The dedicated space must be physically secured from unauthorised access and is not located in a disaster-prone area. A financial institution must also ensure there is no single point of failure in the design and connectivity for critical components of the production data centres, including hardware components, electrical utility, thermal management and data centre infrastructure. A financial institution must also ensure adequate maintenance, and holistic and continuous monitoring of these critical components with timely alerts on faults and indicators of potential issues.
translation: Data centres must have redundant power including UPS systems and backup generators. Generators must be able to sustain operations for at least 72 hours during a power outage. Regular testing of power failover must be conducted.
evidence: Generator capacity and fuel supply records

title: Disaster Recovery Planning
verbatim:
A financial institution shall enhance the resilience of key digital services and delivery channels through the following measures:
(a) implement effective mechanisms by 30 September 2027 to provide early signals or warning of service degradation or intermittent failures. This means the capability to:
(i) detect failed transactions and measure service availability more accurately;
(ii) monitor the number of affected customers and transaction volumes during service disruptions to measure the extent and severity customer impact; and
(iii) immediately escalating to senior management when service disruptions affect 5% or more of expected daily customers or transaction volumes;
(b) conduct regular reviews to identify and mitigate potentially vulnerable IT system interdependencies to prevent simultaneous interruptions to multiple digital services or delivery channels; and
(c) establish stand-in processing arrangement by 30 September 2027 and deploy such arrangement during disruptions to ensure continuity of services during disruptions. A financial institution shall prioritise deployment of this capability for digital services and delivery channels which are least substitutable, and ensure its customers are well informed on the terms of use (such as the transaction limit), associated risks and allocation of responsibilities between parties and mitigation actions against fraud risks.
translation: Every critical system needs a disaster recovery plan covering how to recover it, who is responsible, how to communicate during the crisis, and when to escalate. Plans must be kept up to date and accessible during emergencies — not locked away on the systems that failed.
evidence: Disaster recovery plan documents for critical systems

verbatim:
A financial institution must ensure continuous and proactive monitoring and timely detection of anomalous activities in its technology infrastructure to prevent potential compromise of its security controls or weakening of its security posture. This shall include-
(a) establishing a Security Operations Center (SOC) supported by competent resources and equipped with the necessary tools and technologies for proactive monitoring of its technology security posture;
(b) ensuring the scope of monitoring must cover all critical systems including the supporting infrastructure; and
(c) conducting regular review of its security posture via the conduct of a vulnerability assessment and penetration testing in line with Appendix 5.
translation: Implement comprehensive email security: spam filters, malware scanning, phishing detection, and DLP. Deploy SPF, DKIM, and DMARC on all your domains to prevent attackers from spoofing your email addresses.
evidence: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC configuration records

artifacts.description: Configuration documentation for email security controls and filtering

artifacts.name: Email Authentication Records

artifacts.description: Report on email security metrics including phishing detection rates

title: DLP Controls
verbatim: A financial institution must establish a process to collect, analyse and evaluate cyber threat information in relation to its environment (“cyber threat intelligence”) to promptly detect cyber threats, including data breach incidents and spread of misleading information in relation to the financial institution over the Internet.
translation: Deploy DLP tools to detect and block unauthorised transfer of sensitive data. DLP policies must align with your data classification and cover all major channels: email, web browsing, endpoints, and cloud services.

artifacts.name: DLP Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining data loss prevention requirements aligned with data classification

artifacts.description: Configuration evidence for DLP controls across email, web, endpoint, and cloud

artifacts.description: Records of DLP alerts and incidents with investigation outcomes

title: Vulnerability Assessment
verbatim: A financial institution must establish appropriate response to investigate and respond to flagged anomalous activities based on their level of complexity.
translation: Scan internet-facing systems for vulnerabilities at least monthly and internal systems at least quarterly. Use industry-standard tools, rate the severity of what you find, and fix vulnerabilities within defined timeframes based on their risk level.
evidence: Monthly scan results for internet-facing systems
Quarterly scan results for internal systems

artifacts.description: Procedure for conducting regular vulnerability assessments with defined schedules

artifacts.name: Vulnerability Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Monthly and quarterly vulnerability scan results with risk ratings

artifacts.description: Tracking log of vulnerability remediation against defined timeframes

title: Penetration Testing
verbatim: A financial institution must establish comprehensive cyber crisis management policies and procedures that incorporate cyber-attack scenarios and responses in the organisation's overall crisis management plan, escalation processes, business continuity and disaster recovery planning. This includes developing a clear communication plan for engaging shareholders, regulatory authorities, customers and employees in the event of a cyber incident.
translation: Conduct penetration testing on critical systems at least once a year and before major releases. Use qualified, independent testers. Risk-rate the findings, report them to management, and fix them within agreed timelines.
evidence: Penetration test reports from qualified testers

artifacts.name: Penetration Testing Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining penetration testing scope, frequency, and qualification requirements

artifacts.name: Penetration Test Reports
artifacts.description: Annual penetration test reports from qualified independent testers

artifacts.name: Remediation Tracking Records
artifacts.description: Records of penetration test finding remediation against agreed timeframes

title: Red Team Exercises
verbatim:
A financial institution must establish and implement a comprehensive Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP). The CIRP must address the following:
(a) Preparedness
Establish a clear governance process, reporting structure and roles and responsibilities of the Cyber Emergency Response Team (CERT) as well as invocation and escalation procedures in the event of an incident;
(b) Detection and analysis
Ensure effective and expedient processes for identifying points of compromise, assessing the extent of damage and preserving sufficient evidence for forensics purposes;
(c) Containment and eradication
Identify and implement remedial actions to prevent or minimise damage to the financial institution, contain and remove the known threats and resume business activities;
(d) Recovery
Implement multiple strategies including contingency plans as part of incident recovery to swiftly resume business operations and significantly enhance redundancy and resilience; and
(e) Post-incident activity
Conduct post-incident review incorporating lessons learned and develop long-term risk mitigations.
translation: Consider conducting red team exercises that simulate real-world advanced attacks to test your defences and incident response. Use experienced red team professionals and define clear rules of engagement before starting.
evidence: Red team exercise plan and rules of engagement

artifacts.name: Red Team Rules of Engagement
artifacts.description: Documented rules of engagement for red team exercises

artifacts.name: Red Team Exercise Reports
artifacts.description: Reports from red team exercises with findings and recommendations

artifacts.name: Red Team Improvement Plan
artifacts.description: Improvement plan addressing findings from red team exercises

title: Vulnerability Remediation
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that relevant CERT members are conversant with the incident response plan and handling procedures and remain contactable at all times.
translation: Define clear remediation timelines by severity: critical vulnerabilities within 7 days, high within 30 days, medium within 90 days. If you cannot meet these timelines, you need formal risk acceptance documentation.

artifacts.name: Vulnerability Remediation Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure defining remediation timelines and processes by risk severity

artifacts.name: Remediation Compliance Report
artifacts.description: Report on vulnerability remediation compliance against defined SLTs

artifacts.name: Remediation Exception Records

title: Bug Bounty & Disclosure
verbatim: A financial institution shall establish a secure and reliable out-of-band communication infrastructure for both internal and external stakeholders to ensure continued coordination and communication if the primary communication infrastructure is compromised or rendered unavailable during a crisis.
translation: Consider running a bug bounty or coordinated vulnerability disclosure program so external security researchers can report vulnerabilities they find. Define clear scope, rules, and safe harbour provisions to encourage responsible disclosure.
evidence: Vulnerability disclosure policy or bug bounty program documentation
Program scope and rules of engagement

artifacts.description: Policy for coordinated vulnerability disclosure or bug bounty program

artifacts.name: Bug Bounty Program Rules

title: Threat Intelligence Program
verbatim:
A financial institution must conduct an annual cyber drill exercise to test the effectiveness of its CIRP including the out-of-band communication methods, based on various current and emerging threat scenarios (e.g. social engineering), with the involvement of key stakeholders including members of the board, senior management and relevant third party service providers. The result of the annual cyber drill exercise must be reported to the board in a timely manner. The out-of-band communication methods must also be tested regularly as part of the institutions cyber drill exercises. The test scenarios must include scenarios designed to test-
(a) the effectiveness of escalation, internal and external communication and decision-making processes that correspond to different impact levels of a cyber incident; and
(b) the readiness and effectiveness of CERT and relevant third party service providers in supporting the recovery process.
translation: You need a formal threat intelligence program that gathers, analyses, and acts on cyber threat information relevant to banking. Use multiple intelligence sources and produce actionable intelligence that drives security operations and risk decisions.

artifacts.name: Threat Intelligence Program
artifacts.description: Documented threat intelligence program covering collection, analysis, and dissemination

artifacts.name: Threat Intelligence Reports
artifacts.description: Periodic threat intelligence reports with actionable findings

artifacts.name: Intelligence Source Inventory
artifacts.description: Inventory of threat intelligence sources used by the program

title: Threat Information Sharing
verbatim:
A financial institution shall review its loss provision arrangements to ensure its adequacy to cover cyber incidents based on its scenario analysis of extreme adverse events. Where cyber insurance is adopted to mitigate impact of cyber incidents, the financial institution shall-
(a) ensure that the scope of the insurance policy adequately covers the information security events and types of liability that the financial institution is exposed to;
(b) understand the terms and conditions of the insurance policy in relation to warranties, attestations or any responsibilities of the financial institution, have them reflected in the crisis response policies and procedures as appropriate and ensure that any changes to IT services and control measures do not result in unintended exclusions of cover; and
(c) ensure that any obligations imposed by the insurance policy (such as in relation to appointment of experts and accepting their recommendations during a cyber incident) do not impair its ability to act in the best interest of the financial institution and its customers. The financial institution shall anticipate and adequately manage any conflict of interest that may arise from the objective of the insurer to minimise the cost of its liability under the insurance policy.
translation: Participate in threat information sharing with industry peers, government agencies, and ISACs. Use the Traffic Light Protocol (TLP) or equivalent to handle shared information appropriately and protect the confidentiality of sources.
evidence: Records of information shared and received

artifacts.name: TLP Handling Procedures

artifacts.name: Information Sharing Records
artifacts.description: Records of threat information shared and received through sharing arrangements

title: Threat Hunting
verbatim: A financial institution is required to notify the Bank of cyber incidents in adherence to the Bank's policy documents on Operational Risk Reporting – Part C, Business Continuity Management – Part C, Merchant Acquiring Services – paragraphs 19.25 to 19.26 and any other relevant policy documents specified by the Bank.
translation: Go beyond automated detection by proactively hunting for threats that may have slipped through. Use structured methodologies driven by threat intelligence or specific hypotheses about potential attack vectors in your environment.

title: IoC Management
verbatim: Subject to the applicable data protection laws, a financial institution must share cyber threat intelligence information with the industry on a timely basis via the relevant sharing platforms developed by the Bank, the industry, or law enforcement agencies. In addition, the financial institution must allocate resources to participate in any industry-wide initiatives aimed at improving collective threat intelligence capabilities.
translation: Manage IoCs through a structured process: collect them, validate them, enrich them with context, and feed them into your security tools for automated detection. Keep IoCs current and maintained in a threat intelligence platform.

title: Cyber Situational Awareness
verbatim: A financial institution shall collaborate and cooperate closely with relevant stakeholders and authorities in combating cyber threats.
translation: Stay aware of the cyber threat landscape by continuously monitoring external threats, your own attack surface, and emerging threats to the financial sector. Provide regular situational awareness reports to senior management and the security team.

title: Digital Services Security Controls
verbatim:
Securing digital services is an integral part of financial institution’s risk management. A financial institution must expand its CRF to implement robust technology security controls in providing digital services which assure the following:
(a) adopt and regularly assess the minimum-security controls for respective delivery channels to ensure confidentiality and integrity of customer and counterparty information and transactions. Refer to Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11;
(b) proper authentication of users or devices and authorization of transactions to mitigate impersonation and fraud risk. Refer the minimum-security controls in Appendix 3; and
(c) strong physical control and logical control measures.
translation: All digital channels — internet banking, mobile banking, and payment systems — must have comprehensive security controls covering authentication, authorisation, encryption, session management, and protection against web application attacks.

title: Alternative Controls for Digital Services
verbatim:
In the event that a financial institution has not yet implemented the digital services security controls specified under paragraph 12.1 to 12.9, the financial institution must-
(a) be ready to provide documented explanation of how alternatives measures or mitigations achieve equivalent or superior effectiveness; and
(b) assume the liability of any fraud that occurs due to the gaps arise from the absence of the specified control.
translation: Mobile banking apps must include security features like code obfuscation, root/jailbreak detection, certificate pinning, and secure local storage. Test the security of each release before publishing to app stores.

title: Cyber Resilience Framework Enhancement for Digital Fraud
translation: Secure all APIs with authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, input validation, and encryption. Maintain an inventory of all APIs, document them, and test their security regularly. Shut down deprecated APIs promptly.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Fraud Detection Capabilities
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that its fraud detection capabilities and rules are updated in a timely manner upon detection of new fraud modus operandi in order to prevent fraudulent transactions or account takeover using stolen customer credentials. This must be supported by appropriate risk analytics to improve the accuracy of fraud detection, that includes continuous upgrade of fraud detection capabilities as specified in Appendix 11 on Fraud Detection Standards.
translation: Customer authentication for digital services must match the risk level of the activity. High-risk transactions require MFA. Regularly assess whether your authentication mechanisms are still effective against current threats.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Attendant Risk Mitigation for Digital Services
verbatim:
A financial institution must mitigate any attendant risk arising from its delivery of digital services. This shall include-
(a) adopt secure communication channel to mitigate risk of phishing. Compensating controls shall be adopted when using communication channel prone to phishing exploits;
(b) all customers must be properly informed in advance of new controls to ease adoption and minimise inconvenience; and
(c) practical ways for customers to verify the authenticity of calls made by the financial institution or its appointed outsourced service providers.
translation: High-value and high-risk digital transactions need out-of-band verification — confirming the transaction through a separate channel from the one used to initiate it. Show customers the full transaction details for confirmation before final authorisation.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Empowerment for Digital Fraud Mitigation
verbatim: A financial institution shall empower customers to mitigate digital fraud risks where it is reasonably practical. This shall include offering customer mechanisms to manage activation and de-activation of payment card for card-not-present and overseas transactions. This shall not absolve the financial institution from its liabilities, responsibilities and duty of care to ensure the security of its digital services.
translation: Implement secure session management for digital services: automatic timeouts, securely generated session tokens, protection against session fixation attacks, and controls on concurrent sessions. Never put session tokens in URLs and always invalidate them on logout.

title: Consumer Competency and Awareness
translation: Deploy real-time fraud detection for digital transactions using behavioural analytics, pattern analysis, and anomaly detection. Flag suspicious transactions for review and be able to block them in real time when necessary.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Reporting and Account Management
verbatim:
A financial institution must provide convenient means for customers to report, suspend and re-activate their account swiftly in the event of a suspected fraud, where applicable. This shall require the financial institution to:
(a) offer a secure self-service “kill switch” solution;
(b) ensure that its contact centre is adequately resourced and operating effectively to provide prompt and adequate assistance to customers in distress; and
(c) restore customer access to digital services within a reasonable timeframe upon validation.
translation: When onboarding customers digitally, use security controls like identity verification, liveness detection, document checks, and fraud screening. The level of controls should match the risk of the products being offered.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Additional Measures for Fraud and Data Compromise Incidents
verbatim:
In addition to effective incident handling procedures, a financial institution must adopt additional measures to mitigate risk to customers arising from any incident involving potential fraud or compromise of customer data. This shall include-
(a) heighten monitoring of affected customer accounts;
(b) notify affected customers and provide them with the necessary information to apply mitigating measures and reduce the risk of fraud; and
(c) revoke and re-issue affected user credentials or designated payment instruments, where there is a potential risk of exploitation due to compromised data or other fraudulent activity.
translation: Secure QR code payments and e-wallet services against tampering, overlay attacks, and forgery. Ensure QR codes are validated, transactions are encrypted, and the integrity and authenticity of every QR-based transaction is verified.

title: Technology Audit Scope and Frequency
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that the scope, frequency, and intensity of technology audits are commensurate with the complexity, sophistication and criticality of technology systems and applications.
translation: Create a risk-based IT audit plan covering all key technology areas. The plan must be approved by the audit committee and executed by qualified IT auditors who understand the technology being audited.

title: Annual Technology Audit Plan
verbatim: A financial institution must establish annual review on its technology audit plan that provides appropriate frequency and coverage of critical technology services, third party service providers, material external system interfaces, delayed or prematurely terminated critical technology projects and post-implementation review of new or material enhancements of technology services.
translation: IT auditors must be independent from the teams and systems they audit. They need unrestricted access to all systems, data, and people for their work. The audit function must report directly to the audit committee, not to IT management.

title: Technology Audit Competency
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure the internal audit function must have dedicated technology audit resources with specialised competencies and professionally certified. The technology audit resources shall be adequately conversant with the developing sophisticate of the financial institution’s technology systems, delivery channels and have sound knowledge in the areas audited.
translation: IT auditors should hold relevant certifications (such as CISA, CISSP) and stay current on technology risks and audit standards. Provide and track continuous professional development for all IT audit staff.

title: Technology Audit Advisory Role
verbatim: The technology audit resources may be enlisted to provide advice on compliance and adequacy of control processes during the planning and development phases of new major products, systems, adoption of third party service providers or technology operations. In such cases, the technology auditors participating in this capacity are expected to carefully consider whether such an advisory or consulting role can materially impair their independence or objectivity in performing post-implementation reviews of the products, systems and operations concerned.
translation: Track all IT audit findings through to completion. Management must provide action plans with target dates for each finding. If findings become overdue, escalate them to senior management and the audit committee.

title: Data Centre Resilience and Risk Assessment (DCRA)
verbatim: A financial institution shall appoint a technically competent external service provider to carry out a production data centre resilience and risk assessment (DCRA). The assessment must consider all major risks and determine the current level of resilience of the production data centre. A financial institution must ensure the assessment is conducted at least once every three years or when there is a material change in the data centre infrastructure, whichever is earlier. The assessment shall, at a minimum, include a consideration of whether the requirements in paragraphs 10.24 to 10.28 have been adhered to. For data centres managed by third party service providers, a financial institution shall only rely on independent third party assurance reports provided such reliance is consistent with the financial institution’s risk appetite and tolerance, and the independent assurance has considered similar risks and meets the requirements in this paragraph for conducting the DCRA. The designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 must deliberate the outcome of the assessment.
translation: Get independent security assessments of your critical third-party providers at least yearly. Evaluate their security controls, data protection, and compliance with your contractual requirements. Your risk management team must review the results.
evidence: Independent security assessment reports for critical vendors
Risk management review records of assessments

title: Network Resilience and Risk Assessment (NRA)
verbatim: A financial institution shall appoint a technically competent external service provider to carry out regular network resilience and risk assessments (NRA) and set proportionate controls aligned with its risk appetite. The assessment must be conducted at least once in three years or whenever there is a material change in the network design, whichever is earlier. The assessment must consider all major risks and determine the current level of resilience. This shall include an assessment of the financial institution’s adherence to the requirements in paragraphs 10.36 to 10.43. The designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 must deliberate the outcome of the assessment.
translation: Require critical vendors to hold recognised security certifications like ISO 27001 or SOC 2. Monitor their certification status continuously. If a vendor's certification lapses, trigger a risk reassessment immediately.

title: Technology and Cybersecurity Awareness Education
verbatim: A financial institution must provide adequate and regular technology and cybersecurity awareness education for all staff in undertaking their respective roles and measure the effectiveness of its education and awareness programs. This cybersecurity awareness education must be conducted at least annually and must reflect the evolving cyber threat landscape and emerging risks. Where appropriate, the financial institution shall also include its third party service providers in their relevant training as outlined in paragraph 10.48(i).
translation: All employees, contractors, and third parties with system access must complete mandatory annual security awareness training covering cyber risks, data protection, and your security policies. Track and report on training completion rates.

title: Continuous Training for Technology Staff

subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
title: Simplified Notification for Enhancements
verbatim:
For any enhancements to existing digital services which meet the criteria in Appendix 6, a financial institution shall be subject to a simplified notification requirement and submit the following information in the notification to the Bank:
(a) description of the enhancements to the existing technologies; and
(b) risk assessment of the proposed enhancements, including the impact and measures to mitigate identified risks.
translation: For enhancements to existing digital services that meet the criteria in Appendix 6, a simplified notification process applies. You only need to submit a description of the enhancements and a risk assessment covering impact and mitigation measures.

subsection: Introduction or enhancement to digital services
title: Assurance and Confirmation for Material Enhancements
verbatim:
For any enhancements to existing digital services which are not subject to the simplified notification requirement under paragraph 16.3, a financial institution is required to undertake the following measures prior to notifying the Bank:
(a) engage an independent external party to provide assurance that the financial institution has addressed the technology risks and security controls associated with the digital services or any material enhancement to the digital services. The format of the assurance shall be as set out in Part A of Appendix 7; and
(b) provide a confirmation by the CISO, senior management officer or the chairman of the board or designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 of the financial institution's readiness to provide digital services or implement any material enhancement to the digital services. The format of the confirmation shall be as set out in Part B of Appendix 7.
translation: For digital service enhancements that do not qualify for simplified notification under 16.3, you must: (a) engage an independent external party to provide assurance that technology risks and security controls have been addressed, using the format in Part A of Appendix 7; and (b) obtain a confirmation from the CISO, senior management officer, or board/committee chairman of the institution's readiness, using the format in Part B of Appendix 7.
evidence: Independent external party assurance report (Appendix 7 Part A format)
CISO or senior management readiness confirmation (Appendix 7 Part B format)
Engagement letter or contract with the independent external party
Evidence that assurance and confirmation were completed prior to BNM notification

section: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
title: First-Time Cloud or Emerging Technology Consultation
verbatim:
A financial institution is required to consult the Bank prior to the first-time adoption of a public cloud or emerging technology for critical systems. During the consultation, the financial institution must demonstrate that specific risks associated with the use of these technologies have been adequately considered and addressed to the satisfaction of the Bank, in order to proceed with the adoption of the public cloud for critical systems for the first time. The financial institution shall undertake the following prior to consulting the Bank on its adoption of public cloud for critical systems:
(a) conduct a comprehensive risk assessment of the proposed cloud adoption or emerging technology, including the possible impact and measures to address and mitigate the identified risks. For public cloud, the assessment must follow the risk outlined in paragraph 10.50 and Appendix 10. For emerging technology, it must follow the guideline in Appendix 9. The financial institution shall also adopt the format of the Risk Assessment Report as per Part A of Appendix 7;
(b) provide a confirmation by the CISO, senior management officer or the chairman of the board or designated board-level committee specified in paragraph 8.3 of the financial institution's readiness to adopt public cloud or emerging technology for critical system. The format of the confirmation shall be as set out in Part B of Appendix 7; and
(c) perform a third party pre-implementation review on public cloud or emerging technology. This review shall encompass the areas set out in Appendix 10 (for public cloud) or Appendix 9 (for emerging technology) and Part C of Appendix 7 for higher-risk services, such as those involving the processing or storage of customer information, or cross-border data transmission.
translation: Develop a board-approved cloud strategy that defines your approach to cloud adoption, which service models are approved, and how cloud decisions are governed. Align it with your overall technology strategy and risk appetite.

subsection: Consultation and Notification related to Cloud Services and Emerging Technology
title: Bank Directive on Cloud or Emerging Technology
verbatim: The Bank may at its discretion direct a financial institution to consult the Bank under paragraph 17.1, notify the Bank under paragraph 17.2 or observe any of the guidance in Appendix 10 or Appendix 9 and to explain any deviations from the guidance in Appendix 10 or Appendix 9, including for a non-critical system or when the pre-requisites in the paragraphs 17.1 or 17.2 have not been met. A financial institution must comply with such a directive promptly and to the satisfaction of the Bank.
translation: If you use AI or ML for decision-making, establish a governance framework covering model risk, data quality, bias, explainability, and accountability. AI models that affect customers should be regularly validated to ensure fairness and accuracy.

verbatim: A financial institution must ensure the roadmap for adoption of cloud services or emerging technology (for critical systems and non-critical systems) is included in the annual outsourcing plan submitted to the Bank in adherence with the requirements in the policy document on Outsourcing or IT Profile reporting. The risk assessment as outlined in paragraph 10.50 for cloud services or Appendix 9 for emerging technology must also be documented and made available for the Bank's review as and when requested by the Bank.

title: Gap Analysis and Action Plan
verbatim: A financial institution must perform a gap analysis of existing practices in managing technology risk against the requirements in this policy document and highlight key implementation gaps. The financial institution must develop an action plan with a clear timeline and key milestones to address the gaps identified. The gap analysis and action plan must be submitted to the Bank no later than 90 days after the issuance date of this policy document. Financial institutions that have previously made a submission in accordance with the equivalent provision in the previous version of this policy document are required to maintain continuous compliance by identifying any new gaps against the enhanced or revised requirements in the latest version of this policy document and taking the necessary steps to address such gaps. The updated annual assessment of its level of compliance must be made available to the Bank upon request.
translation: Conduct a formal annual self-assessment of your compliance with the full RMiT policy. For each requirement, determine whether you are fully compliant, partially compliant, or non-compliant, and provide supporting evidence for your assessment.
evidence: Compliance status for each RMiT clause
